title: Rigidity for Boundary Actions and Classification in Low Dimensions
speaker: Federico Rodriguez-Hertz
abstract:
The plan of the talk is to describe joint work with A. Brown and Z. Wang on the smooth classification of actions of lattices in SL(n,ℝ)
 on n−1 dimensional manifolds. The method is also amenable to show rigidity for some boundary actions.
tags: Joint IAS/PU Groups and Dynamics Seminar

proof_stages.label: Low-dimensional rigidity theorem and boundary models
proof_stages.summary: The talk states the main dichotomy for a lattice Γ<SL(n,R) acting C^r on a compact (n−1)-manifold: either the action factors through a finite group, or the manifold and action are the standard projective/spherical boundary ones, described as homogeneous quotients G/Q and G/Q+.

proof_stages.label: Invariant-measure problem and suspension
proof_stages.summary: To overcome the lack of invariant measures for boundary actions, the Γ-action is induced to the suspension M_α=(G×M)/Γ over G/Γ, where amenability of the parabolic P yields a P-invariant, in particular A-invariant, measure projecting to Haar measure.

proof_stages.label: Lyapunov spectrum and resonance
proof_stages.summary: For an A-ergodic measure on the suspension, Oseledets theory decomposes the tangent bundle into base root directions E_ij with weights t_i−t_j and fiber Lyapunov directions F_λ, and a fiber exponent is called resonant when it is a positive multiple of a root.

proof_stages.label: Nonresonance invariance criterion
proof_stages.summary: The key measure-rigidity input is that if a root is nonresonant with every fiber Lyapunov exponent, then the measure is invariant under the corresponding root subgroup; in the purely horizontal case this gives invariance immediately.

proof_stages.label: SL(3) surface dichotomy and finite-action branch
proof_stages.summary: For SL(3) actions on surfaces, only two fiber exponents can be resonant with the three relevant negative roots, so every P-invariant measure is either G-invariant or Q-invariant with two specific resonances; in the G-invariant case cocycle superrigidity and the Brown–Fisher–Hurtado criterion force an isometric, hence finite, action.

proof_stages.label: Root rates in the standard boundary action
proof_stages.summary: The standard projective/spherical boundary action is analyzed in local coordinates to show that its contraction rates are exactly the relevant roots, with proportionality constant one, giving the template that the resonant case must recover.

proof_stages.label: Resonant coarse Lyapunov and conditional-measure geometry
proof_stages.summary: Pesin stable manifolds and their intersections isolate resonant blocks such as E_21⊕F_λ1; conditional measures on these blocks project to Haar horizontally, are atomic vertically, lie on measurable graphs, and recurrence yields affine line invariance forcing the resonance coefficient to equal one.

proof_stages.label: Chart reconstruction and global boundary classification
proof_stages.summary: Combining the two resonant directions gives Haar measure on the graph of a linear map from the horizontal root plane to an open fiber chart, and matching these measure-defined charts with the standard sphere charts gives smooth transition maps and leaves only S^{n−1} or RP^{n−1} globally.

examples.name: Linear toral action of SL(n,Z)
examples.note: The standard action on T^n is used only to contrast a familiar action with many invariant measures against boundary actions with none, so it motivates the invariant-measure problem without producing the proof idea by itself.
examples.engagement_reason: The SL(n,Z) linear action on the torus is introduced as a historical example and only facts like Haar and finite-orbit invariant measures are stated, with no computation or construction carried out on it.

passes.reason: standard algebraic torus action used as a positive invariant-measure model rather than an obstruction
The standard linear SL(n,Z) action on T^n is a highly regular algebraic model at the critical natural-representation dimension, and it is used as a positive invariant-measure example.
The standard linear action of SL(n,Z) on the torus is a highly regular algebraic action used as a benchmark with invariant measures, not to refute a claim.
the standard linear toral SL(n,Z)-action is used as a positive algebraic comparison with invariant measures, not to refute a claim
The standard linear SL(n,Z) action on T^n is a highly regular algebraic model at the critical n-dimensional toral threshold, used as a positive invariant-measure example.
canonical algebraic torus action by the full lattice automorphism group, used as a positive invariant-measure model
standard algebraic SL(n,Z) action on the n-torus, a critical Zimmer-type toral model with high linear regularity used as a positive invariant-measure example

The standard SL(n,Z) action on T^n is the critical algebraic toral action just above the low-dimensional boundary range, used as a contrasting source of invariant measures rather than to refute a claim.
The SL(n,Z) action on T^n is the standard homogeneous linear model, used as a positive comparison with Haar invariant measure rather than as an obstruction.
the standard algebraic torus action sits at the critical dimension n and is used as a source of invariant measures rather than to refute a claim
standard algebraic torus action of the full lattice in the critical minimal dimension, used as a positive contrasting model with invariant measures
standard algebraic torus action in the critical Zimmer dimension n, used as a positive invariant-measure model

examples.name: Hyperbolic element on the circle
examples.note: The SL(2,R) picture with two fixed points illustrates why the projective boundary action has no invariant probability measure, serving the motivational stage rather than the classification proof.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker draws the circle case, analyzes a hyperbolic element's two fixed points, possible invariant measures, and uses conjugation to reason concretely why the boundary action has no invariant measure.

passes.reason: a generic hyperbolic circle/projective-line element is used to obstruct a global invariant measure for the boundary action
A hyperbolic circle element is a structurally typical source-sink Möbius element, used as an obstruction to an invariant measure for the boundary action.
A hyperbolic projective circle element is an ordinary regular element, used to obstruct the existence of a global invariant measure for the boundary action.
the hyperbolic circle element is used as the fixed-point obstruction showing an invariant boundary measure cannot exist
A hyperbolic circle element is structurally ordinary but is used as the fixed-point obstruction showing an invariant measure cannot exist for the boundary action.
used to force invariant measures onto two fixed points and thereby obstruct a global invariant measure
a typical hyperbolic projective circle element is used to obstruct the existence of a common invariant measure via its two fixed atoms
A hyperbolic circle element is a structurally ordinary north-south Möbius/dynamical element, used to obstruct the existence of a group-invariant boundary measure.
A hyperbolic circle element is used to obstruct a common invariant measure for the boundary action via its two fixed-point measures.
the hyperbolic circle element is used to obstruct a global invariant measure for the boundary action, but is otherwise a typical north-south element
used to obstruct a common invariant measure for the boundary circle action via its two fixed-point atomic measures
standard north-south Möbius dynamics used to obstruct a common invariant boundary measure

examples.name: Symplectic-type root-system complications
examples.note: In Q&A, groups such as Sp are mentioned to illustrate that resonances among roots themselves can complicate the nonresonance criterion beyond the split one-dimensional-root-space case.
examples.engagement_reason: Symplectic/SP root-system issues are only cited in a question response as causing resonant-root complications, without working through a specific root system.

passes.reason: classical symplectic root-system resonances are presented as complications obstructing a straightforward extension of the argument
The symplectic-type resonant-root phenomenon is a systematic family complication, not a sporadic exception, and is presented as an obstacle to extending the argument directly.
Symplectic-type root-system resonances are a classical-family complication cited as an obstruction to directly extending the SL(n) argument.
the symplectic/root-resonance case is cited as a structural obstruction to extending the nonresonance argument
The symplectic root-resonance phenomenon is a classical infinite-family complication, cited as an obstruction to a straightforward extension of the argument.
classical symplectic root-system resonances are cited as complications obstructing a direct extension of the argument
resonant symplectic-type root configurations are cited as a classical-family obstruction to naively extending the argument
The symplectic-type/root-resonance case is a classical systematic family, invoked as an obstruction to a straightforward extension of the SL(n) argument.
Symplectic-type root resonances are cited as an enemy case complicating the extension of the argument to other groups.
the symplectic proportional-root situation is cited as an obstruction to extending the proof, with distinct root directions collapsed by resonance
symplectic-type root resonances are cited as an obstruction to straightforwardly extending the root-isolation argument
classical type-C/Sp root-system resonance is an infinite-family obstruction to the simple nonresonance argument

examples.name: Small C0 perturbations of boundary actions
examples.note: In Q&A, small smooth perturbations of a boundary action are cited as an application where the same conditional-measure picture can be repeated to obtain conjugacy back to the original action.
examples.engagement_reason: Small C0 perturbations of boundary actions are cited as an application where the same tricks imply conjugacy, but no particular perturbation is analyzed step by step.

passes.reason: small C0 perturbations are used as a rigidity application, remaining mostly conjugate to the boundary action
Small C0 perturbations form an ordinary nearby perturbative family and are used positively to illustrate rigidity via conjugacy to the boundary action.
Small C0 perturbations of boundary actions are used as a rigidity application, and are not structurally sporadic, limiting, or extremal.
small C0 perturbations are presented as a positive rigidity application, remaining mostly conjugate to the boundary action
Small C0 perturbations of boundary actions are ordinary local-rigidity test cases, presented positively as still measurably conjugate to the original action.
nearby perturbations are presented as a positive rigidity application rather than as failures
small C0 perturbations of boundary actions are used as a positive local-rigidity application rather than as structurally special exceptions
Small C0 perturbations of boundary actions are used as a positive local-rigidity application, and a generic small perturbation is not intrinsically maximally symmetric or exceptional.
Small C0 perturbations of boundary actions are presented as a successful rigidity application, not as a failure case.
small C0 perturbations are presented as a positive rigidity application and are structurally ordinary perturbations of the boundary model
nearby smooth perturbations of boundary actions are used as a positive local-rigidity application rather than a failure case
nearby smooth deformations of boundary actions are used as a positive local-rigidity application
